topic: SEC-2b.7 rewrite verification: temporal note on results.json removal (EXP-069 / ISS-715 / ISS-716)
triggered_by: EXP-069 integration: decider added 'Endpoint removed (2026-04-11)' paragraph to SEC-2b.7
our_argument_summary: Section 2b.7 documents the April 2026 results.json drift from 33/34 to 35/35. The rewrite adds a closing paragraph noting that the /api/current/results.json endpoint now returns 404 as of 2026-04-11, approximately one day after our poller documented its drift. The rewrite claims this removal is 'itself revealing' and that it occurred 'shortly after this review documented its scoring pathologies.' The rest of the section text is unchanged from the c2-reviewed version.
rewrite_scope: Single paragraph appended ('Endpoint removed (2026-04-11)'). No changes to existing paragraphs. ISS-705 W004 identity verification resolved the c2 critical blocker, but the text was NOT patched with the improved notation recommended by ISS-705. Other c2 major/moderate issues also remain unaddressed.

holes_found.description: The new paragraph states: 'the dome appears to have removed the structured scoreboard between 2026-04-10 and 2026-04-11, approximately one day after our poller flagged its drift' and 'The removal is itself revealing: the endpoint was taken down shortly after this review documented its scoring pathologies.' This implies the dome removed results.json BECAUSE of our review. This is a causal inference without evidence. We do not know (a) whether the dome site author is aware of our review, (b) whether the removal was planned independently, or (c) whether the removal was part of a broader API reorganization. The temporal correlation (our analysis ≈ removal timing) does not establish causation. A dome defender can say: 'We removed results.json as part of a scheduled API migration. Your obscure review site was not a factor. This is conspiratorial thinking — exactly the kind of inference you criticize us for making.' This defense is strong because we ourselves criticize the dome for post-hoc causal inference (e.g., relabeling existing physics as dome-predicted). Applying the same inferential standard to ourselves: temporal proximity alone does not prove responsive removal.
holes_found.recommendation: Separate the factual claim (endpoint returns 404) from the causal inference (removed because of our review). Replace 'The removal is itself revealing: the endpoint was taken down shortly after this review documented its scoring pathologies' with something like: 'Whether the removal was prompted by our analysis or by an independent API change, the effect is the same: the structured scoreboard that showed 100% by construction is no longer available for independent verification. The evidence on which this section is based was captured at fetch time and is preserved in our project record.' This framing preserves the analytical point (the scoreboard is gone, our cached data stands) without claiming causation we cannot demonstrate. If we later obtain evidence of causation (e.g., dome commit message referencing our review), we can strengthen the claim then.

holes_found.description: The c2 review (SEC-2b.7.c2.json) identified two major issues that remain unpatched in the rewritten text: (1) The 'direction_correct AND snr_sufficient' scoring criterion is stated as fact without citing its source — was it read from dome code, inferred from field names, or documented on the dome site? (2) The Finlay et al. 2020 citation claims 1-3 nT uncertainties 'translate to a nonzero angular uncertainty on any derived separation quantity' without showing the error propagation math. Both were flagged as major in the c2 review. The rewrite only added the endpoint-removal paragraph without addressing either.
holes_found.recommendation: These should be addressed in a follow-up patch: (1) For the scoring criterion, the ISS-705-verification.json confirms the field names direction_correct and snr_sufficient are actual fields in results.json, so cite that: 'results.json includes fields direction_correct and snr_sufficient; entries where both are true receive auto_verdict: confirmed_strong, as observed in the W004 entry.' (2) For Finlay et al., either show the error propagation or (simpler and more robust) replace the specific citation with the general argument: 'No geophysical measurement has zero uncertainty; TASK-3-1's noise_floor=0 and sigma_distance=0 indicate the observation was copied from the model, not measured independently.' This is stronger because it does not depend on a citation-to-quantity extrapolation.

holes_found.description: ISS-705-finding.json recommended changing 'Scoreboard split on the same observation (W004 / WIN-025)' to explicitly note the dome's dual numbering scheme. The current text does include '(note: results.json uses its own W-number namespace distinct from the wins-page sidebar)' — which partially addresses the concern — but the parenthetical notation '(results.json entry W004, our WIN-025)' is still potentially confusing to a reader who looks up 'W004' on the dome wins page and finds 'Noise Floor Limit.' The ISS-705-finding recommended: 'results.json entry W004 (the dome's internal API label for the 2024 Eclipse 9-Station Replication, corresponding to our WIN-025).' This makes the cross-reference unambiguous. The namespace note in italics is easy to miss.
holes_found.recommendation: Apply the ISS-705-finding notation patch: change 'results.json entry W004, our WIN-025 — the 2024 Eclipse 9-Station Replication' to 'results.json entry W004 (the dome's internal API label for the 2024 Eclipse 9-Station Replication — corresponding to our WIN-025, not to be confused with wins-page WIN-004 which refers to Noise Floor Limit).' This makes the namespace collision explicit in the first reference rather than relying on a reader noticing the italicized note.

holes_found.description: The new paragraph says 'the dome appears to have removed the structured scoreboard between 2026-04-10 and 2026-04-11.' The ISS-705-verification.json confirms the endpoint was live on 2026-04-10T18:12:25Z (a successful raw.githubusercontent.com fetch). The ISS-715-finding.json confirms 404 on all URL variants on 2026-04-11T07:13:45Z. The time window is approximately 13 hours, not 'between' two calendar days. If the dome committed the removal to GitHub, we could narrow this further by checking the dome's commit history. More precise timing would strengthen the claim and show we are rigorous about temporal evidence — which is important since we criticize the dome for vague temporal claims.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the timing: 'The endpoint was confirmed live at 2026-04-10T18:12 UTC via raw.githubusercontent.com fetch and returned 404 from all tested URL variants by 2026-04-11T07:14 UTC — a removal window of approximately 13 hours.' Add in a footnote or parenthetical: '(Evidence: ISS-705-verification.json timestamped fetch; ISS-715-finding.json 404 confirmation.)' This level of precision matches the standard we hold the dome to.

holes_found.description: C2 review moderate issue: the sign-test reductio claims a -10±2 nT prediction counts as confirmed at -17.6, -30, -100, or -10,000 nT. If results.json has ANY overshoot filtering not reflected in the JSON fields, this reductio fails. The ISS-705-verification.json now provides the actual W004 field dump: overshoot_ratio = 1.76. But the section does not state whether there IS an overshoot threshold. The captured data shows W001 has sigma_distance = 7.2875 and is below_detection_threshold — so high sigma_distance entries get a different auto_verdict. This suggests the scoring rules may be more nuanced than 'direction_correct AND snr_sufficient.' The W001 entry has direction_correct: false and snr_sufficient: false, but its sigma_distance of 7.3 raises the question: would a sigma_distance of 100 with direction_correct: true still pass? We don't know.
holes_found.recommendation: Add: 'W001 (sigma_distance 7.3, direction_correct false) received auto_verdict: below_detection_threshold, demonstrating that high sigma_distance alone does not guarantee a pass — the direction_correct condition is necessary. However, no results.json entry demonstrates an upper bound on acceptable sigma_distance when direction_correct is true. The W004 entry passes at sigma_distance 3.8 (observation 76% outside the stated uncertainty band). Whether an entry with sigma_distance 100 and direction_correct true would also pass remains untested by the dome's scoring rules as expressed in this data file.' This preempts 'you missed our outlier filter' while being honest about what we cannot prove.

holes_found.description: The new paragraph ends: 'Our analysis stands on the captured data.' This is an assertion of confidence, not an argument. It is true that the captured data preserves the evidence, but a dome defender can say: 'Your analysis was based on a snapshot of a file we were in the process of updating. You caught it in a transitional state that does not reflect our intended scoring methodology.' The 'our analysis stands' framing does not address this defense.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'Our analysis stands on the captured data' with: 'The four scoring anomalies documented above — scoreboard split, pred=obs hardcoding, null-vs-null confirmation, and detection-threshold exclusion — were properties of the scoring rules as expressed in the live JSON, not transient data-entry errors. Each anomaly was reproduced across independent fetches on 2026-04-09 and 2026-04-10. The removal of the endpoint does not retroactively change what the rules computed while they were live.' This makes the defense ('transitional state') harder to sustain.

holes_found.description: Carried forward from c2 review: the bold 'Kernel of truth.' label is stylistically inconsistent with sections 2b.1-2b.6 which do not use this label. Minor cosmetic issue.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the bold label and integrate as unlabeled prose, or add kernel paragraphs to all 2b subsections for consistency.

stronger_arguments.description: The endpoint removal could be documented more powerfully by checking the dome's GitHub commit history for the removal commit. If the commit message references our review or mentions 'cleanup' without specifics, either outcome supports our narrative — explicit reference proves awareness, vague 'cleanup' language is consistent with responsive removal masked as maintenance. The commit timestamp would also narrow the removal window precisely.

The W001 entry from ISS-705-verification is even more damning than the section currently presents. W001 predicted -2.1 nT but observed +3.73 nT — the SIGN is wrong (predicted negative, observed positive), yet it is classified as 'below_detection_threshold' rather than 'falsified.' This means the scoring rules have no 'sign-flip failure' category. An observation that is directionally opposite to the prediction is classified as a non-detection rather than a miss. This is more powerful than the current framing ('a non-detection is kept in the total count but excluded from the failure numerator') because it shows the escape hatch actively concealing a directional failure, not just a weak signal.
sources: ISS-705-verification.json W001 entry: prediction -2.1, observed +3.73, direction_correct: false

stronger_arguments.description: The section could note that the dome's API included a discovery document (api/index.json) and that results.json is no longer listed there as of 2026-04-11 (per ISS-715-finding). This shows the removal was a deliberate API surface change, not a broken link or deployment error.
sources: ISS-715-finding.json: 'results.json NOT listed in api/index.json discovery document as of 2026-04-11'

kernel_of_truth.description: The endpoint-removal paragraph is editorially justified. Documenting that the data source has been removed is necessary for intellectual honesty — readers who try to verify our claims by visiting the URL deserve to know it no longer works. The section correctly preserves the provenance chain (cached data, ISS-705-verification timestamps). The overall argument of SEC-2b.7 remains strong: the four scoring anomalies are well-documented and the ISS-705 independent verification confirmed every factual claim.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The paragraph overreaches by implying causation ('the endpoint was taken down shortly after this review documented its scoring pathologies'). A review that holds itself to the same evidential standard it demands of the dome would present the temporal correlation without asserting or implying a causal link. The paragraph is necessary; only its causal framing needs adjustment.

summary: The SEC-2b.7 rewrite correctly adds a temporal note about results.json removal — a necessary update since readers cannot verify the endpoint themselves. However, the new paragraph implies causation (dome removed endpoint because of our review) without evidence, which is hypocritical given that we criticize the dome for post-hoc causal inference. Two c2 major issues (uncited scoring criterion, Finlay et al. extrapolation) remain unaddressed. The ISS-705 notation patch was partially but not fully applied. Overall, the section's argument is sound and all factual claims have been independently verified. Recommended fixes are all minor_edit level — tighten causal language, apply remaining c2 patches, add W001 sign-flip detail.

c2_issue_tracker.critical_w004_identity: RESOLVED by ISS-705. Factual claim correct. Notation partially improved (namespace note present) but ISS-705 recommended patch not fully applied.
c2_issue_tracker.major_scoring_criterion_uncited: STILL OPEN. Text still states 'direction_correct AND snr_sufficient' as fact without citing source.
c2_issue_tracker.major_finlay_extrapolation: STILL OPEN. Finlay et al. 2020 citation still used without error propagation math.
c2_issue_tracker.moderate_reductio_upper_bound: STILL OPEN. No upper-bound check or acknowledgment added.
c2_issue_tracker.moderate_null_vs_null_defense: STILL OPEN. No evidence of persistence across versions added.
c2_issue_tracker.moderate_counts_against_model_math: STILL OPEN. Formula still presented as inferred without explicit marking.
c2_issue_tracker.minor_staleness_note: SUPERSEDED by endpoint removal paragraph. The temporal note is stronger than the recommended 'fetched on [date]' parenthetical.
c2_issue_tracker.minor_kernel_style: STILL OPEN. Bold label still present.

citations_verified: ISS-705-verification.json timestamps (2026-04-10T18:12:25Z fetch, results.json live) — confirmed by reading the file
ISS-715-finding.json 404 confirmation (2026-04-11T07:13:45Z) — confirmed by reading the file
api/index.json discovery document no longer lists results.json — confirmed per ISS-715-finding
citations_unchecked: Whether the dome's GitHub commit history contains a removal commit for results.json (would strengthen or weaken the causal inference)

deeper_analysis:
The SEC-2b.7 rewrite is a necessary but imperfect update. The core analytical work — identifying four scoring anomalies in results.json — was independently verified by ISS-705 and stands on firm ground. The W004/WIN-025 identity crisis from the c2 review is definitively resolved: W004 in results.json IS the eclipse test, confirmed by two independent fetches.

The new paragraph about endpoint removal is factually correct but rhetorically overreaches. We are a review that prides itself on distinguishing correlation from causation (this is central to our criticism of the dome's post-hoc retrodiction pattern). Implying that the dome removed results.json because of our review, without evidence of the dome author's awareness or intent, violates our own methodological standard. The fix is simple: state the temporal facts, note the coincidence, and let the reader draw their own conclusion. 'The endpoint was removed within 13 hours of our last confirmed fetch' is more powerful than 'the endpoint was taken down shortly after this review documented its scoring pathologies' because the former invites the reader to notice the implication while the latter asserts it.

On the other side of the argument: one could argue that the dome author almost certainly IS aware of our review (it's publicly indexed, referenced in LLM-facing metadata, and the dome's own AI infrastructure would likely encounter it). The causal inference may be correct — but 'probably correct' and 'demonstrated' are different standards, and we should hold ourselves to the higher one.

The more important gap is the two unaddressed c2 major issues. The scoring criterion source and the Finlay et al. extrapolation are not things that 'can wait' — they are active attack surfaces that a dome defender can exploit right now. The ISS-705 verification actually provides the data to fix both issues (it dumps the exact field names and confirms direction_correct/snr_sufficient are real fields), so the fix is straightforward. The decider should prioritize these patches in the next cycle.

The W001 sign-flip finding from ISS-705-verification is a significant missed opportunity. The current text frames W001 as 'a non-detection excluded from the failure denominator.' But ISS-705 reveals it predicted -2.1 nT and observed +3.73 nT — the sign is wrong. This is not a below-detection-threshold case; this is a directional miss classified as below-detection to avoid counting against the model. This reframing would make the W001 anomaly much more powerful and should be integrated as a priority edit.
